concerns.text: FR-006 mandates a 'paired t-test comparing the SAA scores of the text-only decomposed pipeline against the baseline MLLM scores reported in the original CiteVQA paper'. This is a methodological impossibility for a paired test. A paired t-test requires two measurements (text-only and MLLM) for EACH of the SAME N data points (QA pairs) to calculate a difference distribution. The 'original CiteVQA paper' reports an aggregate baseline score (a single scalar), not the per-instance SAA scores for the specific 1,897 QA pairs in this project's subset. You cannot pair a new prediction with a published aggregate mean. This renders the statistical claim in SC-002 and FR-006 invalid.

The definition of 'Strict Attributed Accuracy' (SAA) in US-2 and FR-004 creates a circular validation risk regarding the 'predicted chunk ID'. The metric requires the model to predict a chunk ID, which is then mapped to a bounding box to calculate IoU. However, if the model's 'reasoning' (Stage 2) is trained or fine-tuned on the CiteVQA dataset (which contains the ground-truth chunk IDs), the model may simply memorize the mapping between text patterns and chunk IDs rather than performing genuine cross-modal spatial reasoning. The spec does not explicitly mandate a 'held-out' test set or a 'zero-shot' evaluation protocol to ensure the model hasn't seen the specific chunk-to-box mappings during training, which would make the SAA score a measure of memorization rather than spatial reasoning capability.

User Story 3 (Visual-Only Control) describes feeding a model 'only the cropped image of the ground-truth region'. This design flaw invalidates the control's purpose. If the model receives the *ground-truth* region, it is trivially given the correct location. The control should test if the model can *identify* the correct region from the image alone (e.g., via object detection or visual QA) without text. As written, the 'Visual-Only' experiment measures the model's ability to answer a question given the answer's location, not its ability to localize the answer. This fails to isolate the 'modality gap' regarding localization capability.
